ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Thai basil chicken rice as a composite dish. FOODON_00002372 (chicken dish) is used as the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat dishes — composite prepared dish with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai basil chicken rice. A00LC (chicken meat dishes) is used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage of prepared ethnic dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of basil chicken rice weighs approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and 150 g of stir-fried basil chicken topping.

nutrient_source.title: 705D Basil Chicken Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry 705D for Basil Chicken Rice (九层塔鸡肉配米饭). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Confidence 0.95 as assigned by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil chicken rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white jasmine rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate source, consistent with the dish's primary carbohydrate component. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 26.5) / 100 ≈ 19.1, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 15.12 × 3.5 = 52.9 g net available carbohydrate adjusted for fibre ≈ 26.5 g net. Estimation is conservative and consistent with published rice-based dish GI data.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 324 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range (200–400 mg/100 g); potassium at 182 mg/100 g falls below the 200 mg/100 g offset threshold and dietary fibre at 1.0 g/100 g is below 3 g/100 g, so no offset credit applies — patients with hypertension should request reduced-sodium sauces, ask for sauce on the side, and limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice as primary carbohydrate source despite moderate total carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate is moderate at 15.1 g/100 g with low sugar (1.6 g/100 g), but the primary carbohydrate source is high-GI white jasmine rice (GI ~72); patients with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or reduce the rice portion, monitor total carbohydrate per serving, and note that oyster sauce and fish sauce may contribute small amounts of added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Total fat is moderate at 4.0 g/100 g with estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.0 g/100 g, which is acceptable, but sodium at 324 mg/100 g is a meaningful cardiovascular risk factor; patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium preparation, limit sauce quantity, and consider increasing vegetable portions to improve potassium and fibre intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory basil and garlic offset by high sodium and high-GI rice
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Holy basil and garlic provide polyphenol-rich anti-inflammatory compounds, and saturated fat is low (~1.0 g/100 g); however, sodium at 324 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema and high-GI white rice in large portions can drive systemic inflammation — patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced-sodium sauces and consider a smaller rice portion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil Chicken Rice (九层塔鸡肉配米饭) is a Thai-origin dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and food courts, catalogued as item 705D in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. The dish consists of stir-fried minced or sliced chicken cooked in a wok with holy basil (kraphao), garlic, fresh chilli, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and soy sauce, served over a bed of steamed white jasmine rice. The flavour profile is savoury, aromatic, and mildly spicy, with the distinctive anise-like fragrance of holy basil dominating the dish. The texture contrasts tender, saucy chicken with fluffy steamed rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 116 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate, with 324 mg sodium — placing it in the moderate-energy, moderate-sodium category. Dietary fibre is low at 1.0 g/100 g. The dish is suitable for most healthy adults but requires sodium management for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The high-GI white rice base means glycaemic management is important for individuals with type 2 diabetes. Holy basil and garlic contribute polyphenols with anti-inflammatory properties. A standard hawker serving is approximately 350 g, delivering roughly 406 kcal and 1,134 mg sodium per plate.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry 705D for Basil Chicken Rice, which is a canonical approved primary source for Singapore hawker dishes. HPB assigned confidence 0.95. All core macros and key micronutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white jasmine rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry 705D as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: the dish is prepared by stir-frying with vegetable oil and contains no hydrogenated fat ingredients; trans fat is confirmed negligible. 3. saturated_fat_g of 1.0 g/100 g is estimated from the fat composition of chicken and vegetable oil components consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for similar preparations; HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this entry. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry 705D for this dish as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white jasmine rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the approved GI literature. 6. Glycemic load per 350 g serving is approximately 19.1, calculated using available carbohydrate per serving. 7. The dish is classified as Thai cuisine but is a Singapore hawker adaptation; multilingual names cover English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai as required by the G6 multilingual rule for a Thai-origin dish served in Singapore. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely true based on ingredients (chicken, vegetable oil, plant aromatics, soy-based sauces); formal halal certification status of specific hawker stalls is not verified by this record.
